verses.text: Rachel envies her sister and demands of Jacob: give me children, or else I die. Jacob's anger flares: am I in God's place, who has withheld from you the fruit of the womb? Both of them are aiming their grief at the wrong person.

Rachel envies her sister and demands of Jacob: give me children, or else I die. Jacob's anger flares: am I in God's place, who has withheld from you the fruit of the womb? Both of them are aiming their grief at the wrong person.
Rachel offers her maid Bilhah, the same way Sarai once offered Hagar. Through her I will be built up. Bilhah conceives. The same old wound, the same old attempted fix.
Rachel offers her maid Bilhah, the same way Sarai once offered Hagar. Through her I will be built up. Bilhah conceives. The same old wound, the same old attempted fix.
Rachel offers her maid Bilhah, the same way Sarai once offered Hagar. Through her I will be built up. Bilhah conceives. The same old wound, the same old attempted fix.
Rachel names the first son Dan — judge. God hath judged me, and hath also heard my voice. She frames God as her courtroom; the child is the verdict.
Bilhah has a second son. Rachel names him Naphtali — wrestling. With great wrestlings I have wrestled with my sister, and prevailed. The sons are scoreboard markers in a sister-war.
Bilhah has a second son. Rachel names him Naphtali — wrestling. With great wrestlings I have wrestled with my sister, and prevailed. The sons are scoreboard markers in a sister-war.

Leah now stops bearing — and joins the same strategy. She gives her maid Zilpah to Jacob. Zilpah has a son. Leah names him Gad — meaning a troop, or fortune. Even the wife with four sons is still anxious.

Leah now stops bearing — and joins the same strategy. She gives her maid Zilpah to Jacob. Zilpah has a son. Leah names him Gad — meaning a troop, or fortune. Even the wife with four sons is still anxious.

Leah now stops bearing — and joins the same strategy. She gives her maid Zilpah to Jacob. Zilpah has a son. Leah names him Gad — meaning a troop, or fortune. Even the wife with four sons is still anxious.
Zilpah's second son she names Asher — happy. Happy am I, for the daughters will call me blessed. Leah is still trying to talk her way into joy.
Zilpah's second son she names Asher — happy. Happy am I, for the daughters will call me blessed. Leah is still trying to talk her way into joy.

Reuben finds mandrakes — a plant believed to help conception — and brings them to Leah. Rachel asks for some. Leah's old wound erupts: is it a small thing you took my husband? Now my son's mandrakes too? They strike a bargain — Rachel will send Jacob to Leah for the night.

Reuben finds mandrakes — a plant believed to help conception — and brings them to Leah. Rachel asks for some. Leah's old wound erupts: is it a small thing you took my husband? Now my son's mandrakes too? They strike a bargain — Rachel will send Jacob to Leah for the night.
Jacob comes home from the field, and Leah meets him: come in to me, for I have hired you with my son's mandrakes. He went in. The household is now openly transactional.
God hearkens to Leah and gives her a fifth son: Issachar — hire. God has given me my hire, she says — because I gave my maid to my husband. Even Leah's gratitude is shaped by the rivalry.
God hearkens to Leah and gives her a fifth son: Issachar — hire. God has given me my hire, she says — because I gave my maid to my husband. Even Leah's gratitude is shaped by the rivalry.
A sixth son: Zebulun — dwelling. Now will my husband dwell with me, because I have born him six sons. Her longing for closeness is in every name.
A sixth son: Zebulun — dwelling. Now will my husband dwell with me, because I have born him six sons. Her longing for closeness is in every name.
Afterward she bears a daughter, Dinah. The text gives Dinah a name but no naming-speech. Daughters in this culture were not counted as currency. Genesis names her anyway.

And God remembered Rachel. He listened to her and opened her womb. She bears Joseph — God has taken away my reproach. May the LORD add to me another son. Her first prayer of gratitude is also a request for the next gift.

And God remembered Rachel. He listened to her and opened her womb. She bears Joseph — God has taken away my reproach. May the LORD add to me another son. Her first prayer of gratitude is also a request for the next gift.

And God remembered Rachel. He listened to her and opened her womb. She bears Joseph — God has taken away my reproach. May the LORD add to me another son. Her first prayer of gratitude is also a request for the next gift.
Once Joseph is born, Jacob asks Laban for leave to go home. Give me my wives and children, for whom I have served you. Twenty years away from Bethel is enough.
Once Joseph is born, Jacob asks Laban for leave to go home. Give me my wives and children, for whom I have served you. Twenty years away from Bethel is enough.
Laban insists Jacob stay. I have learned by experience that the LORD has blessed me for your sake. Name your wages — anything. He has noticed exactly whose God is blessing his household.
Laban insists Jacob stay. I have learned by experience that the LORD has blessed me for your sake. Name your wages — anything. He has noticed exactly whose God is blessing his household.

Jacob's reply lays out the math. When I came, you had a little; now you have much. The LORD has blessed you because of me. When shall I provide for my own house? Twenty years of work, and Jacob still has no flock of his own.

Jacob's reply lays out the math. When I came, you had a little; now you have much. The LORD has blessed you because of me. When shall I provide for my own house? Twenty years of work, and Jacob still has no flock of his own.

Don't give me anything, says Jacob. Instead: let me keep the speckled, spotted, and dark-coloured among the flocks. Send the rest of them with your sons. My honesty will be its own witness — anything not speckled in my fold will count as stolen.

Don't give me anything, says Jacob. Instead: let me keep the speckled, spotted, and dark-coloured among the flocks. Send the rest of them with your sons. My honesty will be its own witness — anything not speckled in my fold will count as stolen.

Don't give me anything, says Jacob. Instead: let me keep the speckled, spotted, and dark-coloured among the flocks. Send the rest of them with your sons. My honesty will be its own witness — anything not speckled in my fold will count as stolen.

Laban agrees — and immediately removes every existing speckled animal himself, putting them three days' journey away under his own sons' care. Jacob is left with the unmarked flock and the unspoken expectation that he will get nothing.

Laban agrees — and immediately removes every existing speckled animal himself, putting them three days' journey away under his own sons' care. Jacob is left with the unmarked flock and the unspoken expectation that he will get nothing.

Laban agrees — and immediately removes every existing speckled animal himself, putting them three days' journey away under his own sons' care. Jacob is left with the unmarked flock and the unspoken expectation that he will get nothing.

Jacob takes branches of poplar, almond, and plane, peels white stripes into the bark, and sets them in the water troughs where the flock comes to drink. The animals mate in front of the striped rods and bring forth striped and speckled young.

Jacob takes branches of poplar, almond, and plane, peels white stripes into the bark, and sets them in the water troughs where the flock comes to drink. The animals mate in front of the striped rods and bring forth striped and speckled young.

Jacob takes branches of poplar, almond, and plane, peels white stripes into the bark, and sets them in the water troughs where the flock comes to drink. The animals mate in front of the striped rods and bring forth striped and speckled young.

Jacob separates his marked flock and faces them toward the rest. He breeds the strong animals with his method and lets the weak ones go to Laban. Within a few years he is exceedingly wealthy — flocks, servants, camels, donkeys — even before he leaves Laban's house.
